coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent Si/CDs for highly sensitive Hg2+ sensing in water environment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Hao
Peng, Liping
Wu, Ning
Liu, Bingqing
Wang, Mingyue
Chen, Yuan
Pan, Zhilan
Liu, Yinsheng
Yang, Wu
coredata.dc:description: A blue fluorescent Si/CDs is successfully synthesized via a conventional solvothermal method, which can serve as a sensitive fluorescent sensor to detect Hg2+ ions in water. The synthesized Si/CDs material exhibits excellent optical properties, high chemical and structural stability in an aqueous solution. Under natural light, the mixture is a colorless suspension while under ultraviolet light, it can emit bright blue fluorescence and the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are 377 and 464 nm, respectively. Particularly, Hg2+ can lead to nearly complete fluorescence quenching of the material suspension and the emission intensity of the constructed fluorescent sensing system linearly decreases with increasing Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–200 μM with a detection limit of 26.7 nM (S/N = 3). The proposed method has been successfully applied in detection of Hg2+ content in environmental and drinking water samples. In addition, the test paper strips based on fluorescent Si/CDs are also prepared by an immersion method for rapid and quantitative detection of Hg2+ in water environment through a visual mode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Si/CDs
Hg2+
The test paper

Test paper preparation and Hg2+ ion detection application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of the Si/CDs and detection mechanism for Hg2+.
(a) SEM images of Si/CDs and (b) EDS images of Si/CDs.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) FT-IR spectrum of Si/CDs.
(a) Excitation (purple) and emission spectra (blue) of Si/CDs, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of Si/CDs solution under different excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 410 nm.
Influences of pH (a), NaCl concentrations (b) and storage days (c) on the fluorescence intensity of Si/CDs suspension.

(a) Photoluminescence spectra of Si/CDs in different cationic aqueous solutions. (b) CIE chromaticity diagram before and after Hg2+ addition, Black (X1 = 0.157, Y1 = 0.196), Hg2+ (X2 = 0.246, Y2 = 0.242), (c) Comparison of emission spectra before and after adding Hg2+. (d) Emission spectra of Si/CDs in the presence of different Hg2+ concentrations (0–500 μM). (e) SV curve in Hg2+ concentration range of 0–200 μM. (f) Anti-interference experiment of Si/CDs.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent Si/CDs for highly sensitive Hg2+ sensing in water environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province; College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, P R China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A blue fluorescent Si/CDs is successfully synthesized via a conventional solvothermal method, which can serve as a sensitive fluorescent sensor to detect Hg2+ ions in water. The synthesized Si/CDs material exhibits excellent optical properties, high chemical and structural stability in an aqueous solution. Under natural light, the mixture is a colorless suspension while under ultraviolet light, it can emit bright blue fluorescence and the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are 377 and 464 nm, respectively. Particularly, Hg2+ can lead to nearly complete fluorescence quenching of the material suspension and the emission intensity of the constructed fluorescent sensing system linearly decreases with increasing Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–200 μM with a detection limit of 26.7 nM (S/N = 3). The proposed method has been successfully applied in detection of Hg2+ content in environmental and drinking water samples. In addition, the test paper strips based on fluorescent Si/CDs are also prepared by an immersion method for rapid and quantitative detection of Hg2+ in water environment through a visual mode.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution caused by heavy metal ions has become a serious global health problem [1]. Particularly, Hg2+ ion, as a typical heavy metal ion, can be transmitted and circulated in the food chain, causing serious harm to environment and human health [2,3]. Hg2+ ion is also a highly toxic carcinogen and distributes in a wide range of aquatic environments, which is produced by a variety of human activities such as coal burning, electronics manufacturing, battery production, mining and garbage incineration [4,5]. In addition, due to the high toxicity, bioaccumulation and recycling of mercury ions, its pollution has seriously threatened environmental safety and public health, and attracted worldwide attention [6,7]. Hg2+ ions spread widely in soil, water, air and even the human body. Hg2+ poisoning can cause a number of serious health problems because it can pass through the outer skin, respiratory system and cell membranes, causing DNA damage, mitotic disorders and the central nervous system damage to some extent [6,8–10]. Besides, mercury poisoning can still lead to liver disease and kidney function failure [11]. Thus, World Health Organization (WHO) stresses that the maximum pollution level of Hg2+ in drinking water is 6 ppb (30 nM) and the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) requires the limit of Hg2+ to be 2 ppb (10 nM) [11]. Therefore, it is necessary to find a simple and economical method to detect the content of Hg2+ ions in water. At present, many methods can be widely used to detect Hg2+ in water, such as cold atomic absorption spectrometry [12], colorimetry [13], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [14], electrochemistry [15] and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [16]. Nevertheless, these methods mentioned above have some limitations, and they usually require complex sample pre-processing and expensive instrumentations [9,17–22]. To make up for the above limitations, it is necessary for researchers to develop some simpler, faster and lower cost analytical methods for sensitively sensing Hg2+.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, have exhibited numerous advantages such as optical stability, good water solubility, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, easy modification and small size [23–25]. There are two main ways to synthesize carbon dots: top-down and bottom-up. The top-down synthesis methods are mainly through arc discharge [26], laser candle burning [27], electrochemical oxidation [28,29] and reflux extraction [30] while the bottom-up synthesis methods mainly include the microwave-assisted synthesis [31], hydrothermal method [32] and solvothermal method [33]. Besides, CDs are deemed more environment-friendly comparing with traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dye [34], which has been playing critical roles in various fields such as biochemistry and biomedicine [30], cell imaging [34,35] and chemical and biological sensors [36,37]. Nowadays, many studies have reported the preparation of CDs using biomass as raw material, such as eggs [38], fruit meat [39], fruit peels [40,41], vegetables [42] and milk [43]. Because these substances have a plenty of proteins, carbohydrates, sugars and other organic matter, they are fascinating raw materials that can be employed to prepare CDs rich in heteroatoms [44–46]. Although the research on the preparation and application of CDs is becoming more and more popular, there are still many limitations about CDs synthesis and applications. For example, due to aggregation in solid or powder state their fluorescence is usually weakened or even completely quenched. It is fortunate that some operations can be exploited to overcome these drawbacks including Si-doping [46] and test paper preparation [47,48]. The paper test is simple and convenient in qualitative detection, which has been used effectively in the detection of Hg2+ in water [49]. So far, a variety of fluorescent test papers and sensors for Hg2+ assay have been developed, including polymers and organic small molecules [50,51]. However, traditional sensing materials still have certain defects, such as high toxicity, low photostability and poor solubility in water environment, which limit their further application. Therefore, more exploratory efforts need to be done to accelerate the development of more durable and cheaper fluorescence sensors to detect Hg2+ ions. Carbon dot (CD) is a new kind of quantum sized luminescent nanoparticles, which has attracted more and more researchers' attention and become a class of fluorescent materials with great development prospects owing to above-mentioned merits.

Herein, we prepared CDs fluorescent material as a sensor to detect Hg2+ content in water and further used the material to prepare a more straightforward qualitative testing paper for Hg2+ detection. The fluorescent material containing Si was synthesized by a solvothermal method and used as a sensing material to determine the content of Hg2+ in the water environment. The prepared Hg2+ fluorescent probe showed a wide linear range (0–200 μM) and a low detection limit (26.7 nM). In addition, an immersion method was used to prepare the test strip for the qualitative detection of Hg2+.

All reagents were analytical grade and used directly without further purification. (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Aqueous solutions of Ca2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Ag+ , Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+ were prepared from corresponding nitrate salts. The citric acid (CA) and methanol (MeOH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Co. Deionized water was prepared in the laboratory.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured with KBr slices using a Digilab FTS-3000 IR spectrometer. Fluorescent spectra were recorded on a FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired on a Bruker X-ray diffractometer (Germany) under CuKα radiation. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on a Zeiss Ultra Plus field emission scanning electron microscope (Germany).

Pure Si/CDs was synthesized through referring to a literature method [52] with a solvothermal method by adding given amounts of APTES and CA into methanol ( Fig. 1). Typically, dissolve 0.57 g APTES and 0.32 g CA in 30 mL methanol, respectively. After the obtained mixture was sonicated for about 20 min it was transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction finished the mixture was cooled naturally to room temperature. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with MeOH and deionized water. Finally, the product was dried at 60 °C for 8 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ in an aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent detection of Hg2+ was performed in deionized water at room temperature by a FluoroMax-4, spectrophotometer with the excitation and emission slit widths of 3 nm. A maximum emission at 464 nm was observed upon excited at 377 nm for the detection system. To evaluate the selectivity and specific response toward Hg2+ of the fluorescent Si/CDs material, different metal ion such as Ca2+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+ and Fe2+ was respectively added in the Si/CDs + Hg2+ solution and corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded. To further explore the sensitivity of the sensor, different concentrations of Hg2+ ions were added to the Si/CDs suspension and the fluorescence intensity changes were measured. Finally, the anti-interference ability of the sensor was also studied by measuring the fluorescence response of the Si/CDs toward Hg2+ in the presence of other metal ions. The concentrations of the detection ion and interference ion were both 1.0 × 10−2 M. After the mixture was homogenized by ultrasound, the fluorescence intensity was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test paper preparation and Hg2+ ion detection application
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent test paper was prepared through soaking the filter paper into 1 mg/mL Si/CDs suspension for 48 h and then drying at 60 ℃ for 8 h. Different concentrations of Hg2+ solutions (1 × 10−5, 1 × 10−4, 1 × 10−3 and 1 × 10−2 M) were detected by the prepared filter paper strips through observing their fluorescence intensity changes under ultraviolet lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in the actual water sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further verify the practicability of the probe, we carried out the spiked Hg2+ recovery test in laboratory tap water and Yellow River water samples from Lanzhou, Gansu Province. Tap water and Yellow River water samples were at first passed through a 0.22 mm filter to remove insoluble impurities, and then a certain amount of Hg2+ was respectively added and the fluorescence intensity was recorded.

The synthesized Si/CDs were characterized using FT-IR, SEM and fluorescence spectra. The morphology images were showed in Fig. 2. As showed in Fig. 2a, Si/CDs showed a flake and spherical morphology. And Fig. 2b exhibited that C, O, Si and N elements were uniformly distributed. C was the main element in the Si/CDs structure. O element resulted from some oxygen-containing groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl and alkoxy functional groups in the Si/CDs surface. The origin of the fluorescence of the Si/CDs was correlated to their surface defects and the assembled geometry of the nanoparticles [53–55].

XRD pattern of Si/CDs was shown in Fig. 3a. A significant peak at 2θ = 22° was observed, which was similar to the CDs previously reported [54]. In order to further study its composition, FT-IR spectrum was measured and displayed in Fig. 3b. The absorption bands at ~3424 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibrations of N-H and -OH/-COOH, and the characteristic peak at ~ 1559 cm−1 was ascribed to wagging vibrations of N-H. The absorbance peak at ~1249 cm−1 was assigned to stretching vibration of C-N bond. The absorption bands between ~1367 to ~1474 cm−1 resulted from scissoring vibration and symmetric bending of Si-CH2. The peaks located at ~1654 cm−1 results from stretching vibration of CO bond. The strong band located at ~1100 cm−1 came from stretching vibration of Si-OR/SiO [53,56]. The results demonstrated the successful synthesis of the target material.

The luminescence properties of Si/CDs suspension were studied at room temperature. The fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of this material were shown in Fig. 4 a. A strong emission could be observed under excitation at 377 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength was 464 nm. Under natural light, the mixture was colorless while under UV-light, it could emit a bright blue fluorescence. At the same time, as shown in Fig. 4 b, the fluorescent peak of Si/CDs suspension blue-shifted with the increasing excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 410 nm, and the fluorescence intensity at first increased then slowly decreased, and reached the maximum value at 370 nm.

The stability of fluorescent Si/CDs suspension was studied under different conditions. Fig. 5 showed fluorescence spectra of the suspension in different pH values, different storage days and different NaCl concentrations. Fig. 5a exhibited that the luminescence of the composite material had a high stability in a wide pH range and it could be used for testing in acidic to alkaline environments. It was noticeable that an acidic medium was conducive to heavy metal ion detection because of inhibition of hydrolysis. From Fig. 5a, it could be seen that the fluorescence intensity slowly increased with increasing pH. It was easily understood because the hydrolysis of Hg2+ ion (Ksp(Hg(OH)2) = 3 × 10−26) was a competitive reaction with its complexation with the surface groups of CDs. As pH increased the enhanced hydrolysis would lead to a decreased stability of the complex of Hg2+ with CDs and as a result, the quenching action of Hg2+ to the fluorescence of the Si/CDs would be weakened. However, this influence was obviously insignificant, especially in neutral medium (the inset of Fig. 5a). Fig. 5b demonstrated that concentration of NaCl solution in the range of 0.2–1.0 mol/L hardly displayed perceptible effect on the fluorescence intensity of the system, indicating that Si/CDs had an excellent salt resistance. When the suspension was stored at room temperature for 7 days, its fluorescence intensity almost kept constant (Fig. 5c) revealing that the luminescence property was stable enough. The above results showed that Si/CDs were stable enough in aqueous solution to serve as a fluorescence chemical sensor.

The synthetic Si/CDs had a series of excellent properties, such as stable blue fluorescence, high fluorescence stability and sensitive response to Hg2+ ions in aqueous solution. When the composite material was dispersed in an aqueous solution of different metal ions (Ca2+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, and Hg2+) the fluorescence spectra at room temperature were showed in Fig. 6a. It could be seen that the fluorescence intensity of Si/CDs decreased significantly after the addition of Hg2+ ions, while addition of other metal ions led to little or even no change. The specific coordinates in the CIE chroma diagram (Fig. 6b) moved from the blue region (0.157, 0.196) to the light blue region (0.246, 0.242) after Hg2+ ion was added. It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity of the Si/CDs suspension at 464 nm was completely quenched in the presence of Hg2+ ions as shown in Fig. 6c and the inset showed a comparison before and after Hg2+ was added under ultraviolet light. These results indicated that Si/CDs had a good selectivity for Hg2+ ions and could be used as fluorescence sensor for recognizing and determining Hg2+ ions. In addition, in order to further evaluate the sensitivity of the Si/CDs sensor, the fluorescence spectra in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ were recorded. As shown in Fig. 6d, the fluorescence intensity of Si/CDs declined gradually with increasing Hg2+ concentration. More importantly, within the concentration range of 0–200 μM, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) to Hg2+ concentration showed a linear relationship (R2 = 0.994) (Fig. 6e). The luminescence quenching effect could be approximately calculated by the Stern-Volmer equation: I0/I = Ksv[M] + C, where Ksv was the quenching constant, [M] was the concentration of Hg2+ ions, I0 and I were the luminescence intensities of Si/CDs suspension in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. The linear equation I0/I = 8.96 × 10−3 [M] + 0.253 was achieved and the detection limit was calculated to be 26.7 nM by the formula LOD = 3Sb/Ksv (LOQ = 89 nM), where Sb represented the standard deviation of the blank measurement. The LOD was lower than the permitted level of WHO, so the Si/CDs sensor also could be applied in the detection of Hg2+ in drinking water samples. The anti-interference of the Si/CDs was further explored and the results were showed in Fig. 6f. It could be found that although the system exhibited slightly different fluorescence intensity in different metal ion solutions, the same concentration of Hg2+ ion completely quenched the fluorescence of the system to nearly the same level in the presence of other metal ions, indicating that the material had a high selectivity for Hg2+ and was not affected by other co-existing interfering substances. In addition, the analytical performance of the Si/CDs fluorescence sensor was compared with those of the previously reported Hg2+ fluorescence sensors (Table S1). The results showed that this method possessed a higher sensitivity and wider linear range, indicating that the Si/CDs fluorescence sensor toward Hg2+ detection in water environment had a good application prospect.

The application of Si/CDs sensor in visual detection of Hg2+ through test paper was studied and the results were showed in Fig. S1. The test paper was prepared by immersing filter paper strips into Si/CDs aqueous suspension (1 mg/mL) for 48 h and drying for 8 h at 60 ℃. Under natural light the test paper was white, while under UV-light it showed a blue fluorescence. When immersed in the Hg2+ solution, the blue-fluorescence of these strips gradually became lighter with the concentration of Hg2+ increasing. Therefore, the paper test method could be effectively applied in the actual sample detection, which was more convenient and rapid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in the actual water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of Si/CDs as Hg2+ sensor was further discussed, and the Hg2+ content in tap water and Yellow River water samples was detected by standard addition method. The different concentrations of Hg2+ (20, 40, 60 μM) standard solutions were spiked into pretreated Yellow River water and tap water samples containing 1 mg/mL Si/CDs. When the suspensions were ultrasonically dispersed uniformly at room temperature, their fluorescence responses were recorded. The determined results, recovery rates and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were listed in Table S2. It could be seen that the recovery rates of Hg2+ were 99.80~103.8% and 99.38~104.55% and RSDs were 1.66~2.83% and 1.59~2.91% in tap water and Yellow River samples, respectively. The above results indicated that the Si/CDs sensor could be successfully applied in the detection of Hg2+ in actual water samples.

In order to explore the fluorescent quenching mechanism of Si/CDs by Hg2+, XRD spectra of the samples before and after treatment with Hg2+ were compared and analyzed (Fig. S2). The results showed that the XRD pattern of Si/CDs after immersion in Hg2+ solution was obviously different from that before immersion, indicating that the structure of the material after immersion in Hg2+ solution has been destroyed. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching may be attributed to the structure destruction and assembled morphology change of Si/CDs caused by the addition of Hg2+. The SEM image after Hg2+ ion treatment as showed in Fig. S3 exhibited that the material morphology has changed from lamellar structure to dense block. Hg2+ led to a structural contraction. At the same time, EDS distribution further demonstrated the existence of Hg2+ ions. In addition, fluorescence spectra of Hg2+ solutions with different concentrations in absence of Si/CDs at the same excitation wavelength as Si/CDs (λex = 377 nm) were measured for comparison. As shown in Fig. S4 Hg2+ exhibited a weak emission peak at 430 nm, suggesting Hg2+ could absorb the excitation energy. In addition, absorption spectra of Hg2+ solution also showed a wear absorption peak between 300 and 500 nm (Fig. S5), indicating that it was likely for Hg2+ to accept transferred energy from Si/CDs. These results implied a strong interaction between Hg2+ and Si/CDs, which led to competitive excitation energy absorption of Hg2+ and the Si/CDs and a non-radiative energy transfer from Si/CDs to Hg2+ and the resulted fluorescent quenching [3]. Due to much lower fluorescent intensity than Si/CDs suspension, influence of Hg2+ emission on its detection was almost negligible.

In conclusion, a novel fluorescent Si/CDs was synthesized by a solvothermal method for the determination of Hg2+ in the water environment. The Si/CDs material exhibited high chemical stability. The constructed fluorescent sensing platform possessed excellent selectivity and high sensitivity for detection of Hg2+ ions with a low detection limit of 26.7 nM. At the same time, a portable and convenient test paper was also prepared through a simple immersion method and applied in rapid visual detection of Hg2+ in environmental water sample. This material was a promising Hg2+ sensing material in environmental monitoring.

H. Guo: Funding acquisition. P.L. Peng: Writing – review & editing. N. Wu and B.Q. Liu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. M.Y. Wang: Formal analysis and software. Y. Chen: Project administration. Z.L. Pan and Y.S. Liu: Investigation and Resources. W. Yang: Writing – original draft and Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21665024), Northwest Normal University Young Teachers Research Capacity Promotion Plan (NWNU-LKQN-18-23) and Key Lab of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province for their financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.128023.

Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective recognition of Hg2+ ion in water by a functionalized metal–organic framework (MOF) based chemodosimeter
Inorg. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Samanta, A.V. Desai, S.V. Sharma, P. Chandra, S.K. Ghosh. Selective recognition of Hg2+ ion in water by a functionalized metal–organic framework (MOF) based chemodosimeter, Inorg. Chem. 57 (2018) 2360–2364.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Terbium(III)/gold nanocluster conjugates: the development of a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for mercury(II) and a paper-based visual sensor
Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.X. Qi, M. Zhang, A.W. Zhu, G.Y. Shi. Terbium(III)/gold nanocluster conjugates: the development of a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for mercury(II) and a paper-based visual sensor, Analyst 16 (2015) 5363–5790.

J. Mater. Chem. C

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembly of alternating copolymer vesicles for the highly selective, sensitive and visual detection and quantification of aqueous Hg2+
Chem. Eng. J.

Sens. Actuators B Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A phenylamine-oligothiophene-based fluorescent chemosensor for selective detection of Hg(II)
J. Fluoresc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent turn-on detection of Hg(II) via the quenching of an iridium(III) complex by Hg(II)-mediated silver nanoparticles
Sci. Rep.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A "turn-on" fluorescent chemosensor for the detection of Hg(II) in buffer-free aqueous solution with excellent selectivity
J. Lumin.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on the distribution of Hg in the environment and its human health impacts
J. Hazard. Mater.

Sens. Actuators B Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot green synthesis of high quantum yield oxygen-doped, nitrogen-rich, photoluminescent polymer carbon nanoribbons as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of silver(I) and mercury(II) ions
